examples.title: PCA: Principal Component Analysis
examples.description: Basic dimensionality reduction using PCA to reduce 3D data to 2D, demonstrating variance-based projection.
examples.code:
import numpy as np
from sklearn.decomposition import PCA
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# Generate synthetic 3D data (elongated cloud)
np.random.seed(42)
n_samples = 300
# Create correlated data - more variance in first two directions
X = np.random.randn(n_samples, 3) @ np.diag([5, 2, 0.1]) + np.array([0, 0, 0])

print("Original data shape:", X.shape)
print("Original variance per axis:", np.var(X, axis=0))

# Apply PCA to reduce from 3D to 2D
pca = PCA(n_components=2)
X_reduced = pca.fit_transform(X)

print("\nReduced data shape:", X_reduced.shape)
print("Explained variance ratio:", pca.explained_variance_ratio_)
print("Total variance retained: {:.2%}".format(sum(pca.explained_variance_ratio_)))

# Visualization
fig = plt.figure(figsize=(12, 4))
ax1 = fig.add_subplot(121, projection='3d')
ax1.scatter(X[:, 0], X[:, 1], X[:, 2], c=np.arange(n_samples), cmap='viridis', alpha=0.6)
ax1.set_title('Original 3D Data')
ax1.set_xlabel('X1'); ax1.set_ylabel('X2'); ax1.set_zlabel('X3')

ax2 = fig.add_subplot(122)
ax2.scatter(X_reduced[:, 0], X_reduced[:, 1], c=np.arange(n_samples), cmap='viridis', alpha=0.6)
ax2.set_title('PCA Reduced to 2D')
ax2.set_xlabel('PC1'); ax2.set_ylabel('PC2')

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
examples.explanation:
1. Generate synthetic 3D data with different variance along each axis
2. PCA computes eigenvectors of the covariance matrix
3. Projects data onto top-k principal components (directions of max variance)
4. Shows how PCA preserves the structure with most variance (first 2 components retain ~97% variance)

examples.title: t-SNE: Non-linear Manifold Learning
examples.description: Advanced manifold learning using t-SNE to visualize high-dimensional data in 2D, preserving local structure.
examples.code:
import numpy as np
from sklearn.manifold import TSNE
from sklearn.datasets import load_digits
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# Load digit dataset (1797 samples, 64 features = 8x8 images)
digits = load_digits()
X, y = digits.data, digits.target

print("Dataset shape:", X.shape)
print("Number of classes:", len(np.unique(y)))

# Apply t-SNE for 2D visualization
# Note: For larger datasets, consider using perplexity=30-50 and more iterations
tsne = TSNE(
    n_components=2,
    perplexity=30,
    learning_rate='auto',
    init='pca',
    random_state=42,
    n_iter=1000
)

X_tsne = tsne.fit_transform(X)

print("t-SNE output shape:", X_tsne.shape)
print("KL divergence (lower is better):", tsne.kl_divergence_)

# Visualization with colored clusters
plt.figure(figsize=(10, 8))
scatter = plt.scatter(X_tsne[:, 0], X_tsne[:, 1], c=y, cmap='tab10', alpha=0.7, s=20)
plt.colorbar(scatter, ticks=range(10))
plt.title('t-SNE Visualization of Handwritten Digits (64D -> 2D)')
plt.xlabel('t-SNE Dimension 1')
plt.ylabel('t-SNE Dimension 2')
plt.show()
examples.explanation:
1. Load digits dataset (64-dimensional feature space)
2. t-SNE converts pairwise distances in high-dim space to probabilities
3. Uses Student t-distribution in low-dim space to preserve local neighborhoods
4. Minimizes KL divergence between distributions using gradient descent
5. Result: clusters of similar digits appear close together, showing manifold structure

examples.title: ISOMAP: Geodesic Distance Manifold Learning
examples.description: Advanced manifold learning using ISOMAP to capture geodesic distances on curved manifolds, demonstrating Swiss Roll unwrapping.
examples.code:
import numpy as np
from sklearn.manifold import Isomap
from sklearn.datasets import make_swiss_roll
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from mpl_toolkits.mplot3d import Axes3D

# Generate Swiss Roll dataset (classic manifold learning example)
X, t = make_swiss_roll(n_samples=1000, noise=0.2, random_state=42)

print("Swiss Roll shape:", X.shape)
print("Manifold dimension: 2 (unrolled)")

# Apply ISOMAP (k-nearest neighbors for graph, then MDS)
isomap = Isomap(n_neighbors=10, n_components=2)
X_isomap = isomap.fit_transform(X)

print("Reconstruction error:", isomap.reconstruction_error())

# Visualization
fig = plt.figure(figsize=(14, 5))

# Original Swiss Roll
ax1 = fig.add_subplot(131, projection='3d')
scatter1 = ax1.scatter(X[:, 0], X[:, 1], X[:, 2], c=t, cmap='viridis', s=10, alpha=0.8)
ax1.set_title('Original Swiss Roll (3D)')
ax1.view_init(10, 70)

# True unrolled manifold
ax2 = fig.add_subplot(132)
ax2.scatter(t, X[:, 1], c=t, cmap='viridis', s=10, alpha=0.8)
ax2.set_title('True Unrolled Manifold')
ax2.set_xlabel('Position along roll'); ax2.set_ylabel('Spread')

# ISOMAP result
ax3 = fig.add_subplot(133)
scatter3 = ax3.scatter(X_isomap[:, 0], X_isomap[:, 1], c=t, cmap='viridis', s=10, alpha=0.8)
ax3.set_title('ISOMAP: Unwrapped Swiss Roll (2D)')
ax3.set_xlabel('ISOMAP Dimension 1'); ax3.set_ylabel('ISOMAP Dimension 2')

plt.colorbar(scatter3, ax=ax3, label='t')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

# Compare with PCA (linear method - fails on curved manifolds)
from sklearn.decomposition import PCA
pca = PCA(n_components=2)
X_pca = pca.fit_transform(X)

fig2, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(12, 5))
axes[0].scatter(X_pca[:, 0], X_pca[:, 1], c=t, cmap='viridis', s=10, alpha=0.8)
axes[0].set_title('PCA (Linear) - Fails to Unwrap')
axes[0].set_xlabel('PC1'); axes[0].set_ylabel('PC2')

axes[1].scatter(X_isomap[:, 0], X_isomap[:, 1], c=t, cmap='viridis', s=10, alpha=0.8)
axes[1].set_title('ISOMAP (Manifold) - Successfully Unwraps')
axes[1].set_xlabel('ISOMAP 1'); axes[1].set_ylabel('ISOMAP 2')

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
examples.explanation:
1. Swiss Roll is a 2D manifold embedded in 3D - PCA fails because it's linear
2. ISOMAP works in 3 steps:
   - Build k-NN graph from data points
   - Compute shortest path geodesic distances (approximating true manifold distances)
   - Apply MDS (Multidimensional Scaling) to find low-dim embedding
3. Result: ISOMAP correctly unwraps the roll, preserving the intrinsic 2D structure
4. Comparison shows why manifold learning is essential for non-linear data
examples.timeComplexity: O(n³) or O(n² log n) with knn
